content.title: Exploration Strategies in Deep Reinforcement Learning
content.title_en: Exploration Strategies in Deep Reinforcement Learning

inlines.text: is a critical topic in Reinforcement Learning. We’d like the RL agent to find the best solution as fast as possible. However, in the meantime, committing to solutions too quickly without enough exploration sounds pretty bad, as it could lead to local minima or total failure. Modern
that optimize for the best returns can achieve good exploitation quite efficiently, while exploration remains more like an open topic.

I would like to discuss several common exploration strategies in Deep RL here. As this is a very big topic, my post by no means can cover all the important subtopics. I plan to update it periodically and keep further enriching the content gradually in time.
As a quick recap, let’s first go through several classic exploration algorithms that work out pretty well in the multi-armed bandit problem or simple tabular RL.
: The agent does random exploration occasionally with probability
and takes the optimal action most of the time with probability
: The agent selects the greediest action to maximize the upper confidence bound
is the average rewards associated with action
is a function reversely proportional to how many times action
: The agent draws actions from a
(softmax) over the learned Q values, regulated by a temperature parameter
: The agent keeps track of a belief over the probability of optimal actions and samples from this distribution. See
The following strategies could be used for better exploration in deep RL training when neural networks are used for function approximation:
into the loss function, encouraging the policy to take diverse actions.
: Add noise into the observation, action or even parameter space (

Good exploration becomes especially hard when the environment rarely provides rewards as feedback or the environment has distracting noise. Many exploration strategies are proposed to solve one or both of the following problems.

The “hard-exploration” problem refers to exploration in an environment with very sparse or even deceptive reward. It is difficult because random exploration in such scenarios can rarely discover successful states or obtain meaningful feedback.
is a concrete example for the hard-exploration problem. It remains as a few challenging games in Atari for DRL to solve. Many papers use Montezuma’s Revenge to benchmark their results.
The “Noisy-TV” problem started as a thought experiment in

. Imagine that an RL agent is rewarded with seeking novel experience, a TV with uncontrollable & unpredictable random noise outputs would be able to attract the agent’s attention forever. The agent obtains new rewards from noisy TV consistently, but it fails to make any meaningful progress and becomes a “couch potato”.

attrs.alt: An agent is rewarded with novel experience in the experiment. If a maze has a noisy TC set up, the agent would be attracted and stop moving in the maze. (Image source: OpenAI Blog: "Reinforcement Learning with Prediction-Based Rewards" )

inlines.text: One common approach to better exploration, especially for solving the
problem, is to augment the environment reward with an additional bonus signal to encourage extra exploration. The policy is thus trained with a reward composed of two terms,
is a hyperparameter adjusting the balance between exploitation and exploration.
reward from the environment at time
, defined according to the task in hand.
This intrinsic reward is somewhat inspired by

). Exploration driven by curiosity might be an important way for children to grow and learn. In other words, exploratory activities should be rewarding intrinsically in the human mind to encourage such behavior. The intrinsic rewards could be correlated with curiosity, surprise, familiarity of the state, and many other factors.
Same ideas can be applied to RL algorithms. In the following sections, methods of bonus-based exploration rewards are roughly grouped into two categories:
Improvement of the agent’s knowledge about the environment.

If we consider intrinsic rewards as rewarding conditions that surprise us, we need a way to measure whether a state is novel or appears often. One intuitive way is to count how many times a state has been encountered and to assign a bonus accordingly. The bonus guides the agent’s behavior to prefer rarely visited states to common states. This is known as the
function that tracks the real number of visits of a state
for exploration directly is not practical, because most of the states would have
, especially considering that the state space is often continuous or high-dimensional. We need an non-zero count for most states, even when they haven’t been seen before.

(Context Tree Switching) density model to estimate pseudo-counts. The CTS model takes as input a 2D image and assigns to it a probability according to the product of location-dependent L-shaped filters, where the prediction of each filter is given by a CTS algorithm trained on past images. The CTS model is simple but limited in expressiveness, scalability, and data efficiency. In a following-up paper,
improved the approach by training a PixelCNN (
The density model can also be a Gaussian Mixture Model as in

. They used a variational GMM to estimate the density of trajectories (e.g. concatenation of a sequence of states) and its predicted probabilities to guide prioritization in experience replay in off-policy setting.
Another idea to make it possible to count high-dimensional states is to map states into
so that the occurrences of states become trackable (

) to convert continuous, high-dimensional data to discrete hash codes. LSH is a popular class of hash functions for querying nearest neighbors based on certain similarity metrics. A hashing scheme

is locality-sensitive if it preserves the distancing information between data points, such that close vectors obtain similar hashes while distant vectors have very different ones. (See how LSH is used in
is a type of computationally efficient LSH and it measures similarity by angular distance:
is a matrix with each entry drawn i.i.d. from a standard Gaussian and
is an optional preprocessing function. The dimension of binary codes is

leads to higher granularity and fewer collisions.

attrs.alt: Algorithm of count-based exploration through hashing high-dimensional states by SimHash. (Image source: Tang et al. 2017 )

inlines.text: For high-dimensional images, SimHash may not work well on the raw pixel level.
designed an autoencoder (AE) which takes as input states
to learn hash codes. It has one special dense layer composed of
sigmoid functions as the latent state in the middle and then the sigmoid activation values
of this layer are binarized by rounding to their closest binary numbers
as the binary hash codes for state

attrs.latex: \mathcal{L}(\{s_n\}_{n=1}^N) = \underbrace{-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^N \log p(s_n)}_\text{reconstruction loss} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \frac{\lambda}{K} \sum_{n=1}^N\sum_{i=1}^k \min \big \{ (1-b_i(s_n))^2, b_i(s_n)^2 \big\}}_\text{sigmoid activation being closer to binary}

inlines.text: One problem with this approach is that dissimilar inputs
may be mapped to identical hash codes but the AE still reconstructs them perfectly. One can imagine replacing the bottleneck layer

, but then gradients cannot be back-propagated through the rounding function. Injecting uniform noise could mitigate this effect, as the AE has to learn to push the latent variable far apart to counteract the noise.

The second category of intrinsic exploration bonuses are rewarded for improvement of the agent’s knowledge about the environment. The agent’s familiarity with the environment dynamics can be estimated through a prediction model. This idea of using a prediction model to measure
was actually proposed quite a long time ago (
is a great way to approximate how much knowledge our model has obtained about the environment and the task MDPs. It captures an agent’s capability of predicting the consequence of its own behavior,
. Such a model cannot be perfect (e.g. due to partial observation), the error

can be used for providing intrinsic exploration rewards. The higher the prediction error, the less familiar we are with that state.  The faster the error rate drops, the more learning progress signals we acquire.
) sketched an idea of using a forward dynamics prediction model to estimate learning progress and assigned intrinsic exploration reward accordingly.
IAC relies on a memory which stores all the experiences encountered by the robot,

. IAC incrementally splits the state space (i.e. sensorimotor space in the context of robotics, as discussed in the paper) into separate regions based on the transition samples, using a process similar to how a decision tree is split: The split happens when the number of samples is larger than a threshold, and the variance of states in each leaf should be minimal. Each tree node is characterized by its exclusive set of samples and has its own forward dynamics predictor
of an expert is pushed into a list associated with each region. The
is then measured as the difference between the mean error rate of a moving window with offset
and the current moving window. The intrinsic reward is defined for tracking the learning progress:

is the moving window size. So the larger prediction error rate decrease we can achieve, the higher intrinsic reward we would assign to the agent. In other words, the agent is encouraged to take actions to quickly learn about the environment.

attrs.alt: Architecture of the IAC (Intelligent Adaptive Curiosity) module: the intrinsic reward is assigned w.r.t the learning progress in reducing prediction error of the dynamics model. (Image source: Oudeyer, et al. 2007 )

inlines.text: trained a forward dynamics model in the encoding space defined by
. The model’s prediction error at time
is normalized by the maximum error up to time
, so it is always between 0 and 1. The intrinsic reward is defined accordingly:
is necessary, as experiments in the paper have shown that a dynamics model trained directly on raw pixels has
behavior — assigning same exploration bonuses to all the states. In
is learned via an autocoder (AE) and

is one of the output layers in AE. The AE can be statically trained using a set of images collected by a random agent, or dynamically trained together with the policy where the early frames are gathered using

model. Predicting the next state given the agent’s own action is not easy, especially considering that some factors in the environment cannot be controlled by the agent or do not affect the agent. ICM believes that a good state feature space should exclude such factors because

children.text: they cannot influence the agent’s behavior and thus the agent has no incentive for learning them

inlines.text: . By learning an inverse dynamics model
, the feature space only captures those changes in the environment related to the actions of our agent, and ignores the rest.
is expected to be robust to uncontrollable aspects of the environment.

attrs.alt: ICM (Intrinsic Curiosity Module) assigns the forward dynamics prediction error to the agent as the intrinsic reward. This dynamics model operates in a state encoding space learned through an inverse dynamics model to exclude environmental factors that do not affect the agent's behavior. (Image source: Pathak, et al. 2017 )

inlines.text: did a set of large-scale comparison experiments on purely curiosity-driven learning, meaning that only intrinsic rewards are provided to the agent. In this study, the reward is
is crucial to learning forward dynamics, which is expected to be
, making the prediction task more tractable and filtering out irrelevant observation.
Random features (RF): Each state is compressed through a fixed random neural network.
: The probabilistic encoder is used for encoding,
Inverse dynamic features (IDF): The same feature space as used in

All the experiments have the reward signals normalized by a running estimation of standard deviation of the cumulative returns. And all the experiments are running in an infinite horizon setting to avoid “done” flag leaking information.

attrs.alt: The mean reward in different games when training with only curiosity signals, generated by different state encoding functions. (Image source: Burda, Edwards & Pathak, et al. 2018 )

inlines.text: turn out to be quite competitive, but in feature transfer experiments (i.e. train an agent in Super Mario Bros level 1-1 and then test it in another level), learned IDF features can generalize better.
They also compared RF and IDF in an environment with a
on. Unsurprisingly the noisy TV drastically slows down the learning and extrinsic rewards are much lower in time.

attrs.alt: Experiments using RF and IDF feature encoding in an environment with noisy TV on or off. The plot tracks extrinsic reward per episode as the training progresses. (Image source: Burda, Edwards & Pathak, et al. 2018 )

inlines.text: The forward dynamics optimization can be modeled via variational inference as well.
) is an exploration strategy based on maximization of
about the agent’s belief of environment dynamics. How much additional information has been obtained about the forward dynamics can be measured as the reduction in entropy.
be the forward prediction model, parameterized by
be the trajectory history. We would like to reduce the entropy after taking a new action and observing the next state, which is to maximize the following:
While taking expectation over the new possible states, the agent is expected to take a new action to increase the KL divergence (
) between its new belief over the prediction model to the old one. This term can be added into the reward function as an intrinsic reward:
Since it is difficult to compute
directly, a natural choice is to approximate it with an alternative distribution
. With variational lower bound, we know the maximization of
’s parameters associated with the new relief after seeing
. When used as an exploration bonus, it is normalized by division by the moving median of this KL divergence value.
Here the dynamics model is parameterized as a
(BNN), as it maintains a distribution over its weights. The BNN weight distribution
. After applying a second-order Taylor expansion, the KL term
, which is easy to compute, because
is factorized Gaussian and thus the covariance matrix is only a diagonal matrix. See more details in
All the methods above depend on a single prediction model. If we have multiple such models, we could use the disagreement among models to set the exploration bonus (
). High disagreement indicates low confidence in prediction and thus requires more exploration.
proposed to train a set of forward dynamics models and to use the variance over the ensemble of model outputs as
. Precisely, they encode the state space with
and learn 5 models in the  ensemble.

attrs.alt: Illustration of training architecture for self-supervised exploration via disagreement. (Image source: Pathak, et al. 2019 )

inlines.text: is differentiable, the intrinsic reward in the model could be directly optimized through gradient descent so as to inform the policy agent to change actions. This differentiable exploration approach is very efficient but limited by having a short exploration horizon.
But, what if the prediction task is not about the environment dynamics at all? It turns out when the prediction is for a random task, it still can help exploration.
) is a novel framework that injects exploration signals based on a newly introduced,
MDP. The idea of DORA depends on two parallel MDPs:
One is the original task MDP;
The other is an identical MDP but with
: Rather, every state-action pair is designed to have value 0. The Q-value learned for the second MDP is called
. If the model cannot perfectly predict E-value to be zero, it is still missing information.

Initially E-value is assigned with value 1. Such positive initialization can encourage directed exploration for better E-value prediction. State-action pairs with high E-value estimation don’t have enough information gathered yet, at least not enough to exclude their high E-values. To some extent, the logarithm of E-values can be considered as a generalization of
When using a neural network to do function approximation for E-value, another value head is added to predict E-value and it is simply expected to predict zero. Given a predicted E-value
. The RND exploration bonus is defined as the error of a neural network
predicting features of the observations given by a
. The motivation is that given a new state, if similar states have been visited many times in the past, the prediction should be easier and thus has lower error. The exploration bonus is

attrs.alt: How RND (Random Network Distillation) works for providing an intrinsic reward. The features $O_{i+1} \mapsto f_{i+1}$ are generated by a fixed random neural network. (Image source: OpenAI Blog: "Reinforcement Learning with Prediction-Based Rewards" )

inlines.text: Two factors are important in RND experiments:

Non-episodic setting results in better exploration, especially when not using any extrinsic rewards. It means that the return is not truncated at “Game over” and intrinsic return can spread across multiple episodes.

Normalization is important since the scale of the reward is tricky to adjust given a random neural network as a prediction target. The intrinsic reward is normalized by division by a running estimate of the standard deviations of the intrinsic return.
The RND setup works well for resolving the hard-exploration problem. For example, maximizing the RND exploration bonus consistently finds more than half of the rooms in Montezuma’s Revenge.

Different from games in simulators, some RL applications like Robotics need to understand objects and intuitive reasoning in the physical world. Some prediction tasks require the agent to perform a sequence of interactions with the environment and to observe the corresponding consequences, such as estimating some hidden properties in physics (e.g. mass, friction, etc).
found that DRL agents can learn to perform necessary exploration to discover such hidden properties. Precisely they considered two experiments:
— The agent has to interact with the blocks and infer which one is heavier.
— The agent needs to infer how many rigid bodies a tower is composed of by knocking it down.
The agent in the experiments first goes through an exploration phase to interact with the environment and to collect information. Once the exploration phase ends, the agent is asked to output a

action to answer the question. Then a positive reward is assigned to the agent if the answer is correct; otherwise a negative one is assigned. Because the answer requires a decent amount of interactions with items in the scene, the agent has to learn to efficiently play around so as to figure out the physics and the correct answer. The exploration naturally happens.

In their experiments, the agent is able to learn in both tasks with performance varied by the difficulty of the task. Although the paper didn’t use the physics prediction task to provide intrinsic reward bonus along with extrinsic reward associated with another learning task, rather it focused on the exploration tasks themselves. I do enjoy the idea of encouraging sophisticated exploration behavior by predicting hidden physics properties in the environment.
Reward-based exploration suffers from several drawbacks:
Function approximation is slow to catch up.
Knowledge fading, meaning that states cease to be novel and cannot provide intrinsic reward signals in time.
Methods in this section rely on external memory to resolve disadvantages of reward bonus-based exploration.
is better running in an non-episodic setting, meaning the prediction knowledge is accumulated across multiple episodes. The exploration strategy,
), combines an episodic novelty module that can rapidly adapt within one episode with RND as a lifelong novelty module.
Precisely, the intrinsic reward in NGU consists of two exploration bonuses from two modules,
The short-term per-episode reward is provided by an
, a dynamically-sized slot-based memory, and an IDF (inverse dynamics features) embedding function
, same as the feature encoding in
At every step the current state embedding
The intrinsic bonus is determined by comparing how similar the current observation is to the content of
. A larger difference results in a larger bonus.
is a kernel function for measuring the distance between two samples.
is a small constant to keep the denominator non-zero. In the paper,
is configured to be the inverse kernel:
is Euclidean distance between two samples and
is a running average of the squared Euclidean distance of the k-th nearest neighbors for better robustness.

attrs.alt: The architecture of NGU's embedding function (left) and reward generator (right). (Image source: Badia, et al. 2020a )

inlines.text: The long-term across-episode novelty relies on RND prediction error in
are running mean and std dev for RND error
However in the conclusion section of the

“We find that the RND exploration bonus is sufficient to deal with local exploration, i.e. exploring the consequences of short-term decisions, like whether to interact with a particular object, or avoid it. However global exploration that involves coordinated decisions over long time horizons is beyond the reach of our method. "
And this confuses me a bit how RND can be used as a good life-long novelty bonus provider. If you know why, feel free to leave a comment below.
The final combined intrinsic reward is
is a constant maximum reward scalar.
The design of NGU enables it to have two nice properties:
revisiting states that have been visited many times
Later, built on top of NGU, DeepMind proposed “Agent57” (
), the first deep RL agent that outperforms the standard human benchmark on
57 Atari games. Two major improvements in Agent57 over NGU are:
of policies are trained in Agent57, each equipped with a different exploration parameter pair
is the reward discounting factor. It is natural to expect policies with higher
to make more progress early in training, while the opposite would be expected as training progresses. A meta-controller (
) is trained to select which policies should be prioritized.
The second improvement is a new parameterization of Q-value function that decomposes the contributions of the intrinsic and extrinsic rewards in a similar form as the bundled reward:
Q(s, a; \theta_j) = Q(s, a; \theta_j^e) + \beta_j Q(s, a; \theta_j^i)

attrs.alt: A pretty cool illustration of techniques developed in time since DQN in 2015, eventually leading to Agent57. (Image source: DeepMind Blog: "Agent57: Outperforming the human Atari benchmark" )

inlines.text: Instead of using the Euclidean distance to measure closeness of states in episodic memory,
took the transition between states into consideration and proposed a method to measure the number of steps needed to visit one state from other states in memory, named
module. The novelty bonus depends on reachability between states.
At the beginning of each episode, the agent starts with an empty episodic memory

At every step, the agent compares the current state with saved states in memory to determine novelty bonus: If the current state is novel (i.e., takes more steps to reach from observations in memory than a threshold), the agent gets a bonus.
The current state is added into the episodic memory if the novelty bonus is high enough. (Imagine that if all the states were added into memory, any new state could be added within 1 step.)
Repeat 1-3 until the end of this episode.

attrs.alt: The nodes in the graph are states, the edges are possible transitions. The blue nodes are states in memory. The green nodes are reachable from the memory within $k = 2$ steps (not novel). The orange nodes are further away, so they are considered as novel states. (Image source: Savinov, et al. 2019 )

inlines.text: In order to estimate reachability between states, we need to access the transition graph, which is unfortunately not entirely known. Thus,
neural network to predict how many steps separate two states. It contains one embedding network
to first encode the states to feature vectors and then one comparator network
to output a binary label on whether two states are close enough (i.e., reachable within
stores embeddings of some past observations within the same episode. A new observation will be compared with existing state embeddings via
and the results are aggregated (e.g. max, 90th percentile) to provide a reachability score
is a predefined threshold for determining the sign of the reward (e.g.
works well for fixed-duration episodes). High bonus is awarded to new states when they are not easily reachable from states in the memory buffer.
They claimed that the EC module can overcome the

attrs.alt: The architecture of episodic curiosity (EC) module for intrinsic reward generation.  (Image source: Savinov, et al. 2019 )

inlines.text: ) is an algorithm aiming to solve the “hard-exploration” problem. It is composed of the following two phases.

for finding shortest paths in a graph. Indeed, no neural network is involved in phase 1. By maintaining a memory of interesting states as well as trajectories leading to them, the agent can go back (given a simulator is
) to promising states and continue doing

exploration from there. The state is mapped into a short discretized code (named “cell”) in order to be memorized. The memory is updated if a new state appears or a better/shorter trajectory is found. When selecting which past states to return to, the agent might select one in the memory uniformly or according to heuristics like recency, visit count, count of neighbors in the memory, etc. This process is repeated until the task is solved and at least one solution trajectory is found.
The above found high-performance trajectories would not work well on evaluation envs with any stochasticity. Thus,
is needed to robustify the solution via imitation learning. They adopted
, in which the agent is started near the last state in the trajectory and then runs RL optimization from there.
One important note in phase 1 is: In order to go back to a state deterministically without exploration, Go-Explore depends on a resettable and deterministic simulator, which is a big disadvantage.
To make the algorithm more generally useful to environments with stochasticity, an enhanced version of Go-Explore (
Instead of resetting the simulator state effortlessly, the policy-based Go-Explore learns a
and uses that to access a known state in memory repeatedly. The goal-conditioned policy is trained to follow the best trajectory that previously led to the selected states in memory. They include a
) loss to help extract as much information as possible from successful trajectories.
Also, they found sampling from policy works better than random actions when the agent returns to promising states to continue exploration.

Another improvement in policy-based Go-Explore is to make the downscaling function of images to cells adjustable. It is optimized so that there would be neither too many nor too few cells in the memory.

attrs.alt: An overview of the Go-Explore algorithm. (Image source: Ecoffet, et al., 2020 )

inlines.text: (Diverse Trajectory-conditioned Self-Imitation Learning), which shared a similar idea as policy-based Go-Explore above. DTSIL maintains a memory of diverse demonstrations collected during training and uses them to train a trajectory-conditioned policy via
. They prioritize trajectories that end with a rare state during sampling.

attrs.alt: Algorithm of DTSIL (Diverse Trajectory-conditioned Self-Imitation Learning). (Image source: Yijie Guo, et al. 2019 )

inlines.text: The similar approach is also seen in
. The main idea is to store goals with

in memory so that later the agent can revisit these goal states with a goal-conditioned policy repeatedly. In each episode, the agent flips a coin (probability 0.5) to decide whether it will act greedily w.r.t. the policy or do directed exploration by sampling goals from the memory.

attrs.alt: Different components in directed exploration with function approximation. (Image source: Guo, et al. 2019 )

inlines.text: The uncertainty measure of a state can be something simple like count-based bonuses or something complex like density or bayesian models. The paper trained a forward dynamics model and took its prediction error as the uncertainty metric.
) introduces a notion of uncertainty in Q-value approximation in classic
method. Bootstrapping is to approximate a distribution by sampling with replacement from the same population multiple times and then aggregate the results.

Multiple Q-value heads are trained in parallel but each only consumes a bootstrapped sub-sampled set of data and each has its own corresponding target network. All the Q-value heads share the same backbone network.

attrs.alt: The algorithm of Bootstrapped DQN. (Image source: Osband, et al. 2016 )

inlines.text: At the beginning of one episode, one Q-value head is sampled uniformly and acts for collecting experience data in this episode. Then a binary mask is sampled from the masking distribution
and decides which heads can use this data for training. The choice of masking distribution
determines how bootstrapped samples are generated; For example,
is an independent Bernoulli distribution with
, this corresponds to the double-or-nothing bootstrap.
always returns an all-one mask, the algorithm reduces to an ensemble method.

However, this kind of exploration is still restricted, because uncertainty introduced by bootstrapping fully relies on the training data. It is better to inject some prior information independent of the data. This “noisy” prior is expected to drive the agent to keep exploring when the reward is sparse. The algorithm of adding random prior into bootstrapped DQN for better exploration (
) depends on Bayesian linear regression. The core idea of Bayesian regression is: We can

children.text: “generate posterior samples by training on noisy versions of the data, together with some random regularization”

inlines.text: be the Q function parameter and
for the target Q, the loss function using a randomized prior function

attrs.latex: \mathcal{L}(\theta, \theta^{-}, p, \mathcal{D}; \gamma) = \sum_{t\in\mathcal{D}}\Big( r_t + \gamma \max_{a'\in\mathcal{A}} (\underbrace{Q_{\theta^-} + p)}_\text{target Q}(s'_t, a') - \underbrace{(Q_\theta + p)}_\text{Q to optimize}(s_t, a_t) \Big)^2

inlines.text: Options are policies with termination conditions. There are a large set of options available in the search space and they are independent of an agent’s intentions. By explicitly including intrinsic options into modeling, the agent can obtain intrinsic rewards for exploration.
) is such a framework for providing the agent with intrinsic exploration bonuses based on modeling options and learning policies conditioned on options. Let
represent an option which starts from
defines the probability distribution of options we can sample from. And by definition we have
p(s_f, \Omega \vert s_0) = p^J(s_f \vert s_0, \Omega) p^C(\Omega \vert s_0)
While choosing options, we would like to achieve two goals:
Achieve a diverse set of the final states from
Know precisely which state a given option
Combining them, we get mutual information
Because mutual information is symmetric, we can switch
in several places without breaking the equivalence. Also because
is difficult to observe, let us replace it with an approximation distribution
. According to the variational lower bound, we would have
I(\Omega; s_f \vert s_0) \geq I^{VB}(\Omega; s_f \vert s_0)

attrs.latex:
\begin{aligned}
I(\Omega; s_f \vert s_0)
&= I(s_f; \Omega \vert s_0) \\
&= - \sum_{\Omega} p(\Omega \vert s_0) \log p(\Omega \vert s_0) + \sum_{s_f, \Omega} p^J(s_f \vert s_0, \Omega) p^C(\Omega \vert s_0) \log \color{red}{p(\Omega \vert s_0, s_f)}\\
I^{VB}(\Omega; s_f \vert s_0)
&= - \sum_{\Omega} p(\Omega \vert s_0) \log p(\Omega \vert s_0) + \sum_{s_f, \Omega} p^J(s_f \vert s_0, \Omega) p^C(\Omega \vert s_0) \log \color{red}{q(\Omega \vert s_0, s_f)} \\
I(\Omega; s_f \vert s_0) &\geq I^{VB}(\Omega; s_f \vert s_0)
\end{aligned}

attrs.alt: The algorithm for VIC (Variational Intrinsic Control). (Image source: Gregor, et al. 2017 )

inlines.text: can be optimized with any RL algorithm. The option inference function
is doing supervised learning. The prior
is updated so that it tends to choose
can also be fixed (e.g. a Gaussian). Various
will result in different behavior through learning. Additionally,
observed that it is difficult to make VIC with explicit options work in practice with function approximation and therefore they also proposed another version of VIC with implicit options.
conditioned only on the start and end states,

children.text: “Variational Auto-encoding Learning of Options by Reinforcement”

inlines.text: ) relies on the whole trajectory to extract the option context
, which is sampled from a fixed Gaussian distribution. In VALOR:
A policy acts as an encoder, translating contexts from a noise distribution into trajectories

A decoder attempts to recover the contexts from the trajectories, and rewards the policies for making contexts easier to distinguish. The decoder never sees the actions during training, so the agent has to interact with the environment in a way that facilitates communication with the decoder for better prediction. Also, the decoder recurrently takes in a sequence of steps in one trajectory to better model the correlation between timesteps.

attrs.alt: The decoder of VALOR is a biLSTM which takes $N = 11$ equally spaced observations from one trajectory as inputs. (Image source: Achiam, et al. 2018 )

assets.alt: An agent is rewarded with novel experience in the experiment. If a maze has a noisy TC set up, the agent would be attracted and stop moving in the maze. (Image source: OpenAI Blog: "Reinforcement Learning with Prediction-Based Rewards" )
Algorithm of count-based exploration through hashing high-dimensional states by SimHash. (Image source: Tang et al. 2017 )

Architecture of the IAC (Intelligent Adaptive Curiosity) module: the intrinsic reward is assigned w.r.t the learning progress in reducing prediction error of the dynamics model. (Image source: Oudeyer, et al. 2007 )

ICM (Intrinsic Curiosity Module) assigns the forward dynamics prediction error to the agent as the intrinsic reward. This dynamics model operates in a state encoding space learned through an inverse dynamics model to exclude environmental factors that do not affect the agent's behavior. (Image source: Pathak, et al. 2017 )
The mean reward in different games when training with only curiosity signals, generated by different state encoding functions. (Image source: Burda, Edwards & Pathak, et al. 2018 )

Experiments using RF and IDF feature encoding in an environment with noisy TV on or off. The plot tracks extrinsic reward per episode as the training progresses. (Image source: Burda, Edwards & Pathak, et al. 2018 )
Illustration of training architecture for self-supervised exploration via disagreement. (Image source: Pathak, et al. 2019 )

How RND (Random Network Distillation) works for providing an intrinsic reward. The features $O_{i+1} \mapsto f_{i+1}$ are generated by a fixed random neural network. (Image source: OpenAI Blog: "Reinforcement Learning with Prediction-Based Rewards" )
The architecture of NGU's embedding function (left) and reward generator (right). (Image source: Badia, et al. 2020a )
A pretty cool illustration of techniques developed in time since DQN in 2015, eventually leading to Agent57. (Image source: DeepMind Blog: "Agent57: Outperforming the human Atari benchmark" )

The nodes in the graph are states, the edges are possible transitions. The blue nodes are states in memory. The green nodes are reachable from the memory within $k = 2$ steps (not novel). The orange nodes are further away, so they are considered as novel states. (Image source: Savinov, et al. 2019 )
The architecture of episodic curiosity (EC) module for intrinsic reward generation.  (Image source: Savinov, et al. 2019 )
An overview of the Go-Explore algorithm. (Image source: Ecoffet, et al., 2020 )
Algorithm of DTSIL (Diverse Trajectory-conditioned Self-Imitation Learning). (Image source: Yijie Guo, et al. 2019 )
Different components in directed exploration with function approximation. (Image source: Guo, et al. 2019 )
The algorithm of Bootstrapped DQN. (Image source: Osband, et al. 2016 )
The algorithm for VIC (Variational Intrinsic Control). (Image source: Gregor, et al. 2017 )
The decoder of VALOR is a biLSTM which takes $N = 11$ equally spaced observations from one trajectory as inputs. (Image source: Achiam, et al. 2018 )
